coredata.dc:title: Network template-based cross-linked Poly(methyl methacrylate)/tin(IV) oxide nanocomposites for the photocatalytic degradation of MB under UV irradiation
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Materials Research and Technology
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Alkayal, Nazeeha S.
Altowairki, Hanan
Alosaimi, Abeer M.
Hussein, Mahmoud A.
coredata.dc:description: Novel nanocomposites, m-phenylenediamine poly (methyl methacrylate)/tin(IV) oxide(m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2), were synthesized and applied to the degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye under UV light. The nanocomposites were produced using an in situ polymerization process with PMMA and m-phenylenediamine, which was used as a cross-linker. Different ratios of SnO2 nanoparticles were loaded with 2, 5, 10, and 20 wt%. FT-IR, SEM, EDX, XRD, and TGA were used to characterize the new nanocomposites. The photocatalytic properties of methylene blue (MB) dye degradation under UV light were studied. The effects of the parameters of MB concentration, nanocomposite loading, and initial solution pH were investigated. The results clearly showed that the prepared nanocomposite containing 2% and 5% SnO2 had an excellent degradation efficiency compared to the other nanocomposites. We observed that the m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposites, under UV light, showed 99.6% and 95.3% degradation of MB dye within 120 min, respectively. The m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposites are therefore promising catalysts for the treatment of polluted water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic degradation of MB
Novel cross-linked PMMA
PMMA-SnO2 nanocomposites
Ultraviolet radiation

The impact of the SnO2 ratio on the photocatalytic activity
Optimization of the MB dye solution photodegradation
The effect of pH on photodegradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites fabrication.
FTIR spectra for (a) m-PDA-PMMA (b) m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 (c) m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 (d) m-PDA-PMMA3/SnO2 (e) m-PDA-PMMA4/SnO2.
SEM micrographs for (a, b) m-PDA-PMMA (x = 2500 and 10,000), (c, d) m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 (x = 3000 and 2400), (e) m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 (x = 1200), and (f) m-PDA-PMMA4/SnO2 (x = 1200).
EDX spectrum of synthesized of a) m-PDA-PMMA and b) m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposite.
XRD patterns of m-PDA-PMMA, SnO2, and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposite.
TGA c of m-PDA-PMMA and m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites.
Photodegradation of MB dye for the m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 at 120 min under UV light.
Percentage photodegradation of MB in the context of samples with different of SnO2 ratio under UV light.
Photocatalytic degradation of different MB dye concentration for the m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 under UV light at 120 min.
Percentage photodegradation of MB in the presence of m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 under UV light (1 × 10−5 M).
Photocatalytic degradation of different nanocomposites weight under UV light for the m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m -PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 at 120 min.
Percentage photodegradation of 1 × 10−5 M MB photodegradation under UV light in the presence a) 0.01 g of m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and b) 0.005 g of m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2.
Thermal properties of m-PDA-PMMA and m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites.
The degradation of MB by the m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposite is compared to those of previously discussed SnO2 based photocatalysts.

350 W Xe lamp (300 nm UV filter)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The percent degradation efficiency and time estimated for the MB dye degradation at various nanocomposites weights of m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2.

head.ce:title: Network template-based cross-linked Poly(methyl methacrylate)/tin(IV) oxide nanocomposites for the photocatalytic degradation of MB under UV irradiation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, King Abdulaziz University, P.O. Box 80203, Jeddah 21589, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polymer Chemistry Lab., Chemistry Department, Assiut University, Assiut, 71516 Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polymer Chemistry Lab., Chemistry Department, Assiut University, Assiut,71516 Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel nanocomposites, m-phenylenediamine poly (methyl methacrylate)/tin(IV) oxide(m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2), were synthesized and applied to the degradation of methylene blue (MB) dye under UV light. The nanocomposites were produced using an in situ polymerization process with PMMA and m-phenylenediamine, which was used as a cross-linker. Different ratios of SnO2 nanoparticles were loaded with 2, 5, 10, and 20 wt%. FT-IR, SEM, EDX, XRD, and TGA were used to characterize the new nanocomposites. The photocatalytic properties of methylene blue (MB) dye degradation under UV light were studied. The effects of the parameters of MB concentration, nanocomposite loading, and initial solution pH were investigated. The results clearly showed that the prepared nanocomposite containing 2% and 5% SnO2 had an excellent degradation efficiency compared to the other nanocomposites. We observed that the m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposites, under UV light, showed 99.6% and 95.3% degradation of MB dye within 120 min, respectively. The m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposites are therefore promising catalysts for the treatment of polluted water.

ce:section.ce:para: Water contamination is becoming an increasingly important environmental challenge because of increased industrial growth and the growing population. Variable types of water pollutants including trace elements, heavy metals of variable compositions, organic dyes, and biological micro-organisms have been widely detected and removed by using hybrid materials [1–3]. Organic dyes from waste material constitute a significant issue among the various water pollutants because they are stable under light irradiation, at elevated temperatures, and in the presence of other chemicals [4]. Synthetic dyes were first developed in 1865, and now textile industries are using and producing about 1.3 million tons of pigments and dyes every year [5]. Dye waste can cause environmental contamination and is a serious health hazard. Methylene blue (MB) is a cationic dye that is commonly used in fabrics, pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and printing industries. MB can cause skin irritation, allergic dermatitis, mutations, and cancer [6]. Various different approaches, such as chemical, physical, and biological methods, are utilized to remove dyes from water [7], including adsorption, coagulation, flocculation, ion exchange, membrane separation, oxidation, and biodegradation techniques [8–11]. Photocatalysts of variable types show good efficiency in the degradation of organic waste when exposed to UV light [12]. Thanks to their excellent performance in the degradation of various contaminants, semiconductors such as nanometal oxides have become more efficient in the photocatalysis process [13]. Nanomaterials specially metal oxides, including titanium dioxide, zinc oxide, and other oxides, were previously classified as effective photocatalysts under UV-light irradiation [14–27]. Other semi-conductive materials have been also used as efficient photocatalysts [28–29]. Moreover, varieties of functional materials and their applications in the field of environmental protection and polluted water purification have drawn wide attention [30–32].

Tin (IV) oxide (SnO2) is a semiconductor material with large band gaps, which has many benefits and many applications in optoelectronic devices, sensors, solar cells, and catalysis [33]. SnO2 has chemical stability and structure that are extremely like TiO2. Furthermore, SnO2 has no negative health effects and is poorly absorbed by the human body. Because of its great stability and low toxicity, SnO2 can be used in the decomposition of environmentally contaminants in wastewater [34]. However, SnO2 as a single photocatalytic semiconductor has large bandgaps and high recombination rates of photogenerated electron hole pair, decreasing its ability to absorb visible light and promote organic compound degradation. Therefore, scientists have focused on metal ions composites with other metal ions and carbonaceous materials like polyacrylics [35]. The photocatalytic degradation of SnO2 has been improved by mixing SnO2 with other metal oxides or metals, and creating polymer nanocomposites [36–38].

On the other hand, multifunctional hybrid polymer-based composite materials have been widely fabricated and applied in variable types of environmental applications [39–46]. For instance, polymer-derived carbon with an SnO2 aerogel has been used as photocatalyst for MB dye. Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) is a flexible amorphous clear polymer known as acrylic or organic glass, which can be used in skylights, lighting signs, shatterproof glass, and the canopies of aircraft. Moreover, PMMA has the properties of transparency, corrosion resistance, hardness, ultraviolet stability, and low water absorption [47,48]. In comparison to traditional inorganic glass, PMMA is a fragile polymer that is easily scratched, although this can be improved by adding materials such as metals and nanoparticles, or through chemical modification [49–51]. Furthermore, modified PMMA can be applied in various fields, such as in medical, optical, conductivity, sensor, solar, electronic, water treatment, and nanotechnology applications [52]. As An example of a PMMA nanocomposite, photocatalytic study of graphene-SnO2-PMMA nanocomposite was carried out to decompose MB dye with different irradiation levels [53]. A 1,12-diaminododecane cross-linker with a PMMA-multiwall carbon nanotube has been investigated as an adsorbed film, for removal of methyl green dye from aqueous solution [54]. A PMMA/multiwall carbon nanotube nanocomposite with 4,4′–diaminobiphenyl as the cross-linking reagent has been used for selective sensing applications [55]. In a continuance of our process to produce hybrid materials for various types of environmental treatments [56–65], m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) was used as a cross-linker for PMMA, and SnO2 at different ratios was loaded to prepare m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites. These photocatalyst nanocomposites were applied to MB degradation. The new nanocomposite was characterized by FT-IR, SEM, EDX, XRD, and TGA. The UV spectrophotometry for the MB dye application and the impact of various factors, including MB concentration, nanocomposite loading, and initial solution pH, were further examined.

Poly (methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Tetrahydrofuran (THF; 99.5%) was purchased from Fisher Chemical (UK). Tin (IV) oxide nanoparticles (SnO2 < 100 nm) was purchased from Nano-Gate company (Egypt). m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) was purchased from PDH in the UK, and methylene blue dye (MB) from Laboratory Reagent. The chemicals were used without purification or other treatments.

We used a Fourier Transform infrared FTIR spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer) in a wavenumber region (650–4000 cm−1). An energy dispersive spectrometer (EDX) (LEO 440i KV) was used to examine the elemental compositions of the nanocomposites. Morphologies were described using a scanning electron microscope, SEM (Quanta 250). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were studied using a Bruker D8 Advance with Cu Kα radiation (wavelength 1.5418 Å) at 40 kV and 40 mA. The patterns were collected between 2θ of 10° and 80°, and the scan speed was 1.5°/min. The thermogravimetric analysis, TGA, analyzed with the Mettler Toledo instrument was used to explain the thermal degradation behavior of the PMMA nanocomposite samples, using a heating rate of 10 °C/min between 25 and 500 °C in an air atmosphere. The Photochemical Lamp was from Toption Instrument (LTD, CO., 300 W UV–vis lamps (Hg-lamp)), with a wavelength from 250 to 750 nm. We used a Spectro UV-VIS Double Beam PC Scanning Spectrophotometer (Labmed, INC.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the m-PDA-PMMA cross-linked polymer
ce:section.ce:para: The polycondensation technique was used to produce m-PDA-PMMA. First, 1 g of PMMA was dissolved in 35 mL THF and stirred for 30 min; second, 20 w% m-PDA was dissolved in 10 mL THF. After that, the m-PDA solution was added to the PMMA mixture, and it was refluxed for around 6 h (70 °C). Finally, the solution was placed in a Petri dish and allowed to cool at room temperature for at least 24 h.

m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites were synthesized using an in situ polymerization process. First, 1 g of PMMA was dissolved in THF solvent (50 mL), then SnO2 nanoparticles with different ratios (2, 5, 10, 20 wt%) were dispersed through the PMMA solution by stirring, before being ultrasonicated for 10 min. Finally, m-PDA was added as a cross-linker, and the mixture was refluxed for about 5 h at 70 °C with regular shaking. The mixture was then placed into petri dishes and left to dry for 24 h at 25 °C. The materials were collected for photocatalytic dye degradation tests.

The photodegradation of MB dye in distilled water by UV light was carried out using the new nanocomposite. A total of 100 mL from
1 ×
10
− 5
M of MB dye solution was added to 0.01 g of m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2, then the solution was stirred for 30 min in the dark to obtain the adsorption–desorption stability of the dye. The mixture was irradiated and collected at a rate of about 3 mL after every 30 min. Then, the absorbance was recorded using a UV–VIS spectrophotometer at the wavelength range of 300–800 nm. The photodegradation efficiency (D%) was measured using the following equation [66]:
D % =
(
C o − C / C o
)
× 100
o r
D % =
(
A o − A / A o
)
× 100
where Co is the concentration of MB before UV and C is the concentration of MB after UV. The adsorption of the dye is represented by A and Ao.

A designed network template-based cross-linked poly(methyl methacrylate)/tin(IV) oxide nanocomposites (m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2) has been successfully fabricated and applied as an interesting photocatalyst for the photocatalytic degradation of MB under UV irradiation. The fabrication process has been carried out by the reaction of pure PMMA polymer with m-PDA in the presence of variable loading of SnO2 nanoparticles in THF under reflux. The produced nanocomposites were synthesized using an in-situ polymerization process. The interaction between the network polymer matrix and the SnO2 nanoparticles is mainly based on the physical interaction including the hydrogen bonding the Van-Der Waals forces. Table 1 shows the m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites with different loadings of SnO2, and Fig. 1 provides a schematic diagram for the nanocomposites.

Figure 2 illustrates the FT-IR spectra of m-PDA-PMMA and the m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites in the region from 650 cm−1 to 4000 cm−1. The absorption peaks at around 2995 and 2948 cm−1 in m-PDA-PMMA are caused by the C–H stretching vibrations in aromatic and aliphatic compounds, respectively.

According to carbonyl group C]O stretching vibrations, a sharp and intense peak was observed around 1720 cm−1. The aromatic ring's C]C stretching vibration generates a peak around 1490 cm−1. The stretching vibration of N–H is responsible for an absorption range from 3450 to 3370 cm−1. Furthermore, the peak around 1615 cm−1 is due to the N–H bending; this indicates that the carbonyl group of PMMA interacts with the amine group of m-phenylenediamine [51,55].

Surface morphologies, provided by Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) images of m-PDA-PMMA and m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites, are shown in Fig. 3 . In (a) and (b), SEM micrographs of m-PDA-PMMA display rounded micro-pores on the polymer's surface at lower and higher magnifications (x = 2500 and 10,000). The SEM images (c)–(f) clearly indicate that SnO2 (bright dots) was dispersed randomly inside m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposite. With the addition of SnO2, the surface morphology changed significantly, confirming the excellent interaction of SnO2 with the m-PDA-PMMA. SnO2 nanoparticles (bright dots) formed on the surface of the m-PDA-PMMA as a result of the SnO2 loading, causing an increase in pore size and significant deformation of the circular holes.

The chemical composition of m-PDA-PMMA, as well as the presence of SnO2 nanoparticles in m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2, were analyzed using EDX. From the EDX spectrum of the m-PDA-PMMA in Fig. 4 (a), two signals for C and O were confirmed, while the EDX spectrum of the m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposites in (b) exhibited peaks corresponding to the C, O, and Sn elements. The nanocomposites had been successfully prepared, as shown by the EDX pattern.

Figure 5 displays the XRD patterns of the m-PDA-PMMA, SnO2, and m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites. The XRD diffractograms were calculated from 10° to 80° in the 2θ range. The XRD diffractogram of m-PDA-PMMA showed broadened peaks at around 15°, 30°, and 43°. The presence of many sharp peaks indicated that the SnO2 was crystalline and pure. The values were obtained at 26.6°, 33.9°, 37.9°, 51.8°, 54.8°, 57.9°, 61.9°, 64,8°, and 66°, respectively, and their correlating h k l planes were (110), (101), (200), (211), (220), (002), (310), (112), and (301), respectively [38]. All the outstanding peaks have been noticeably specified as the tetragonal phase of SnO2 with the corresponding XRD standard card (JCPDS card no.01-0657).

The XRD patterns of the m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites show peaks in the vicinity 2θ at about 15°, 30°, and 43°, which is due to the m-PDA-PMMA, as well as other sharp peaks confirming that the SnO2 contributed to improved m-PDA-PMMA chain interactions and arrangements in all of the m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites. There was a slight peak position change due to sample thickness, but this had no effect on SnO2 identification.

The thermal stability of the m-PDA-PMMA and m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites was determined using TGA under airflow, with a heating rate of 10 °C/min, to emphasize the impact of SnO2 on the thermal stability of m-PDA-PMMA, as shown in Fig. 6 . Figure 6 indicates that m-PDA-PMMA degrades in three stages [51,67] The first decomposition stage occurred with a maximum at about 225 °C, due to the removal of water.

The weight loss in the second stage ranged from 225 to 300 °C, which could be due to the degradation of the cross-linking bond. The polymer chain was completely decomposed in the third step, which started at 300 °C and finished at 404 °C. T10, T25, and T50 are the decomposition percentages that describe the temperatures for a mass loss of 10%, 25%, and 50%, respectively, as described in Table 2 .

First, the T 10, T25, and T50 of the m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 nanocomposites were higher than for other nanocomposites and m-PDA-PMMA. As the nanocomposites were compared to m-PDA-PMMA, the decomposition temperature T10 and T25 were moved to higher temperatures, for which the order of greater thermal stability was as follows: m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2< m-PDA-PMMA3/SnO2 < m-PDA-PMMA4/SnO2 <m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 < m-PDA-PMMA. The m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA4/SnO2 nanocomposites were also more resistant to degradation at T50.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The impact of the SnO2 ratio on the photocatalytic activity
ce:section.ce:para:
Figures 7 and 8
show that the photoactivity of m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 was determined by the photodegradation of MB dye under UV light for each sample separately. The degradation efficiencies of m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 prepared with 2, 5, 10, and 20% SnO2 were about 99.6, 95.3, 75.5, and 11.6% for the MB dye solution, respectively.

The m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 displayed the highest MB degradation efficiency at 120 min. Several factors affect the photocatalytic dye degradation process, including the type and concentration of organic dye, the amount and type of photocatalyst, and the source and strength of light. Therefore, the photocatalytic dye degradation behavior of the current m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposite was approximately compared to previously published data for SnO2-based catalysts in Table 3 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the MB dye solution photodegradation

ce:section.ce:para: The impact of dye concentration on the photodegradation of MB dye was studied by altering the starting dye concentration 1 × 10−5 M, 2 × 10−5 M and 3 × 10−5 M in the presence of 10 mg of m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposites. According to the findings in Fig. 9 , the degradation rate decreased as the initial dye concentrations were increased, and the optimum concentration of MB was found to be 1 × 10−5 M.
Figure 10 shows the best %D at 1 × 10−5 M, at which the degradation efficiencies of nanocomposites with 2% and 5% SnO2 were 99.6% and 95.3%, respectively.

To investigate the effect of nanocomposite weight on the photoactivity of m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 against MB dye, the weight of both nanocomposites was obtained for 0.005, 0.01, and 0.015 g per 100 mL of 1 × 10−5 M MB dye solution, as presented in Fig. 11 (a and b).

In the case of m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2, which contains 2% SnO2, it was found that when the mass of nanocomposites was 0.01 g, the photocatalytic activity increased to 99.6%. When m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 that contained 5% SnO2 was used, the degradation efficiencies of 0.01 g and 0.015 g were 95.3% and 100% at 120 min, respectively. The 0.005 g m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposite had the highest photodegradation of 100% of the MB dye solution at 60 min. The degradation efficiency and time estimated for the MB dye degradation at various nanocomposite weights of m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 are shown in Table 4 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of pH on photodegradation
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on MB photodegradation in 100 mL of 1 × 10−5 M MB dye solution was investigated further using 0.01 g of m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and 0.005 g m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2. The pH of the MB dye solution was changed from 2 to 10 using diluted HCl and NaOH (0.1 M).

Figure 12 (a) shows the degradation was high in the presence of m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 in a highly acidic medium (pH∼2), with a %D of 98.5% in 60 min and 99.6%, compared to 120 min at a normal pH ∼ 6. (b) In the presence of m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2, the MB degradation was 96% and 100% at 60 min at pH 2 and 6, respectively, and it was also high at pH 4 and 8 at 93.8% and 100% at 120 min, respectively.

In this work, m-PDA-PMMA/SnO2 nanocomposites were synthesized for environmental degradation of MB dye using an in situ polymerization process with variable loads of SnO2, and using m-phenylenediamine as a cross-linker. The new PMMA nanocomposite was characterized using FTIR spectroscopy, EDX, SEM images, XRD, and TGA. The FT-IR results presented the flexible link between PMMA and m-phenylenediamine. The EDX analysis and XRD confirmed the presence of SnO2 in the prepared nanocomposites. SEM demonstrated that micropores were present on the surface m-PDA-PMMA and the surface morphology had changed because of the addition of SnO2, resulting in an increase in pore size. The ordering of increasing thermal stability at T10 and T25 was m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 < m-PDA-PMMA3/SnO2 < m-PDA-PMMA4/SnO2 < m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 < m-PDA-PMMA. The results showed that the m-PDA-PMMA1/SnO2 and m-PDA-PMMA2/SnO2 nanocomposites, which contained 2% and 5% SnO2, respectively, had the highest photoactivity for removing MB from wastewater. The photodegradation process was also influenced by several factors, such as MB concentration, catalyst loading, and the pH of the solution. Finally, the present work will inspire our group as well as some researcher in the field for more developments to get more advantage to the eco-friendly remediation process. In addition to enhance the catalytic performance of such network structures as novel photocatalysts.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Taif University Researchers Supporting Project number (TURSP-2020/244), Taif University, Taif, Saudi Arabia.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Core–shell activated carbon-ZIF-8 nanomaterials for the removal of tetracycline from polluted aqueous solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. A. R. Ahmadi, M. R. Kalaee, O. Moradi, F. Nosratinia, M. Abdouss, Core-shell activated carbon-ZIF-8 nanomaterials for the removal of tetracycline from polluted aqueous solution. Advanced Composites and Hybrid Materials 4, 1384-1397, 2021. doi: https://doi.org/10.1007/s42114-021-00357-3

Puranik, Reactions of 2, 3-Dibromonaphthalene-1, 4-Dione and Pyridyl Amines: X-ray Structures, DFT Investigations, and Selective Detection of the Hg2+ and Ni2+ Ions. Eng. Sci., 14, 78-93, 2021. doi: https://dx.doi.org/10.30919/es8d427

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective removal of nitrates from the drinking water by chemical and electrochemical methods

sb:title.sb:maintitle: SnO2 aerogel deposited onto polymer-derived carbon foam for environmental remediation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. G. Parale et al., SnO2 aerogel deposited onto polymer-derived carbon foam for environmental remediation. Journal of Molecular Liquids, 287, 2019, doi: 10.1016/j.molliq.2019.110990.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review on various types of pollution problem in textile dyeing & printing industries of Bangladesh and recommandation for mitigation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Mia et al., Review on various types of pollution problem in textile dyeing & printing industries of Bangladesh and recommandation for mitigation, Journal of Textile Engineering & Fashion Technology, 5(4), 220-226, 2019, doi: 10.15406/jteft.2019.05.00205.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cationic dye (methylene blue) removal from aqueous solution by montmorillonite

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Khayyam Nekouei, F. Pahlevani, M. Mayyas, S. Maroufi, and V. Sahajwalla, Direct transformation of waste printed circuit boards into high surface area t-SnO2 for photocatalytic dye degradation, Journal of Environmental Chemical Engineering, 7(3), 103133, 2019, doi: 10.1016/j.jece.2019.103133.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ formation of SnO2 nanoparticles on cellulose acetate fibrous membranes for the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Costantino, A. Armirotti, R. Carzino, L. Gavioli, A. Athanassiou, and D. Fragouli, In situ formation of SnO2 nanoparticles on cellulose acetate fi brous membranes for the photocatalytic degradation of organic dyes, Journal of Photochemistry & Photobiology, A: Chemistry, 398, 112599, 2020, doi: 10.1016/j.jphotochem.2020.112599.
